turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We will hear argument first this morning in Case 12짯1226, Young v. United Parcel Service. Mr. Bagenstos.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: If Peggy Young had sought an accommodation for a 20짯pound lifting restriction that resulted from any number of conditions, whether acquired on or off the job, the summary judgment record reflects that UPS would have granted that accommodation. But because Peggy Young's 20짯pound lifting restriction resulted from her pregnancy and not from one of those conditions, UPS rejected her request. That, we submit, is a violation of the second clause of the PDA which, if it means anything, must mean that when an employee seeks an accommodation or benefit due to her pregnancy, that she is entitled to the same accommodation that her employer would have given her.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, what you make it sound as if the only condition that was not accommodated was a lifting restriction because of pregnancy, and I 짯짯 I did not understand that to be the case. I mean, that's the way you start. You want 짯짯 you want me to say it's only pregnancy, unless I've 짯짯 I've missed something.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Well, I 짯짯 so I think on the summary judgment record here, Your Honor, that the three very broad classes of 짯짯 of limitations that UPS accommodates do 짯짯 at least there's a genuine issue of material fact that they cover the waterfront of everything but pregnancy. But our position is that those three broad classes by themselves, even if there are some conditions out there, that they don't cover create 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm sorry. I'm confused.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, I mean, I think that's a necessary starting point for your case. It seems to me that you started out by really giving a misimpression.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Well, I 짯짯 Your Honor, I would submit that that's not right. I would submit that in 짯짯 on this summary judgment record, UPS had 짯짯 UPS acknowledges that they provide accommodations to people with on짯the짯job injuries, but also the summary 짯짯 but also the summary judgment record shows that UPS provides accommodations to drivers with off짯the짯job injuries that result in DOT disqualification, and UPS has not been able to point to a single driver who has a lifting restriction similar to my client, Peggy Young's, who didn't get accommodated who was not pregnant. So 짯짯 so I think 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Bagenstos, what would your case be if 짯짯 let's accept, for argument's sake, that there's a category people who are injured off duty who do not get light work assignments. So you 짯짯 you pointed to three large categories that do, but let's suppose one category doesn't.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Yes. So in that case, our position would be, as the plain text of the statute demands, that the employer would be required to treat the pregnant plaintiff the same as those classes of employees who get accommodations 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Most favored nations treatment.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: So 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It doesn't have to be read that way. It 짯짯 it could be read that way, and it could 짯짯 it could also mean that if you give it to employees generally, you have to give it to pregnant employees, although there may be special 짯짯 special classes. I think one of the briefs had an example about, you know, if 짯짯 if you have your 짯짯 your senior employees driven to work when 짯짯 when 짯짯 when they are unable to drive themselves, you have to do the same for pregnant women. Would you say that that's the case?

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: No, we would not say that.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Why not?

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: We would 짯짯 we would not say that because our position is that the statutory text requires that employers provide workers who are disabled by pregnancy the same treatment they would receive if they themselves had a similar 짯짯 had a condition with a similar effect on the ability to work, but that had a different source. So what the statute prohibits is discrimination based on the source of the workplace limitation, not based on seniority, not based on position within the company.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But suppose you have a 짯짯

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Breyer.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Suppose 짯짯 I mean, we have a brief, as you've seen it, from the truck drivers, and 짯짯 and they say they don't give many of these benefits to anybody. And suppose they do, though, give a benefit to a truck driver who has driven over a particularly difficult mountain pass, you know, where 짯짯 and gotten himself in some danger. Now, the 짯짯 the 짯짯 the harm or the disability is lifting precisely the same. It's just that the source was different. You see, this came from taking a 짯짯 some truck that 짯짯 doing something special with it, and, again, it's a kind of most favored nation problem. I don't know that source gets you out of it. Is 짯짯 is 짯짯 what do you say about that?

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: So I 짯짯 I think as to that, the 짯짯 the important point is that 짯짯 that is an example of what may be an idiosyncratic decision by an employer to provide an accommodation to a particular employee. I think, as Justice Scalia 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, I don't know that it's idiosyncratic, you see, because I don't know all the workplaces, and I can imagine that employers have all kinds of different rules for different kinds of jobs. And 짯짯 and is 짯짯 are you saying as long as there is one job in respect to which, let's say, they give them benefits of $1,000 a week when you're hurt on this job but not on others 짯짯

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: And so 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: 짯짯 then do you have to give them to all pregnant women who hold different jobs? Now, I think the answer to that must be no, but the problem for that and for you is how do you distinguish your situation from that?

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Right. And I 짯짯 and I think actually what Justice Scalia's question to me a minute ago 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: 짯짯 actually contains the seeds of the answer to that.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Exactly.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: So 짯짯 so it seems to me I might agree that an employer that provides a particularly good deal to a single nonpregnant employee doesn't set a 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Not a single. There's a class of people.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: For a small 짯짯 right. So 짯짯 so 짯짯 but when you have an employer that provides to a large class, to 짯짯 to its employees generally, to many of its employees, this accommodated work treatment 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: I see 짯짯 I see that that 짯짯 now, it sounds 짯짯 the other question I have, and it's the only other one, is it did seem to me there is a way, given your theory it's a quite easy way for you to win, and that would be to bring a disparate impact claim, and that's what I thought disparate impact claims were about.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: So 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But you didn't bring the disparate claim and, therefore, what am I to do because I don't know that you want to twist the disparate, you know, intent claim out of shape when you have such a beautiful vehicle to bring a claim of the kind you just articulated.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Well, I think the vehicle to bring the claim of the kind that I articulated is the second clause of the PDA. The second clause of the PDA says that, "Women affected by pregnancy, childbirth, or related conditions shall be treated the same as other persons not so affected but similar in their ability or inability to work."

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, you read that as a 짯짯 JUSTICE KAGAN : Could that take 짯짯

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: You read that as an accommodation provision basically, and maybe 짯짯 maybe it is. But let me ask you this question, which goes to the 짯짯 the issue of 짯짯 of whether the types of accommodations that you would say are required have to meet some reasonableness standard. Let's say there are two categories of employees who have lifting restrictions in their job descriptions. One consists of people who work alone and they lift all the time. A driver who is driving a truck by herself and has to lift heavy packages all the time would fall into that category. The second category would consist of people who lift more occasionally, and they do it in a place where there will always be lots of other employees in the same class available to do the lifting. Now, if an accommodation is provided to the workers in the second category, would you say that one must also be provided to workers in the first?

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: No, I don't think so. So our point is precisely that a driver who is pregnant and who has a limitation related to her pregnancy is entitled to the same accommodation her employer would have given her if she had sought it for a different medical condition with the same effect on the ability to work.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: But why 짯짯 why doesn't that fit 짯짯 why doesn't that second class fit within your reading of the statutory text?

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Well, so because, in our 짯짯 in our view, the statutory text, by saying 짯짯 by drawing this distinction between employees affected by pregnancy, childbirth, and related medical conditions and not so affected, saying employers can't draw that distinction, excuse me, and saying instead they look only at the ability to work, what it does is it prohibits discrimination based on the source of the disabling condition. It doesn't prohibit discrimination based on different job classifications. If you have a driver 짯짯 if an employer says no driver who drives alone is going to get an accommodation whether for an on짯the짯job injury, a DOT disqualifying injury, or pregnancy, that's fine because it's the same treatment. Because, Justice Alito, we do not read this statute as an independent, reasonable accommodation 짯짯

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But why then 짯짯 I guess I'm not quite understanding why you can 짯짯 get the source classifications into a different category from all other classifications. So explain that to me.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Well, so, I mean, I think it 짯짯 it goes to the statutory text. So, I mean, the statutory text says, "Women affected by pregnancy, childbirth, or related medical conditions shall be treated the same," et cetera, "as other persons not so affected but similar in their ability to work." What that 짯짯 what that text is saying is 짯짯 to an employer, don't consider whether this person is affected by pregnancy or not so affected. That's not the basis on which you can compare this employee to other employees. Instead, compare this employee based on ability to work. And remember, this statutory text was adopted in response to General Electric v. Gilbert which upheld an employer policy that distinguished based on the source of the disabling condition, treated some kinds of disabling conditions differently from pregnancy짯related disabling conditions.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: I think you're reading 짯짯 you have admitted that other persons can't really be read literally. Other 짯짯 you have to read things into it. And you would read into it people in the same job classification. But if you can do that, then why can't you also read into it people whose injuries, whose disabilities have the same source?

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Because once you do that, then the second clause of the PDA doesn't occupy any space and then 짯짯 and Congress should have stopped with the first clause. Congress was doing something with the second clause. It was trying to overturn the Gilbert situation, where you had an employer that adopted a policy that, as a formal matter, treated pregnant people the same way it treated nonpregnant people. If you were pregnant, but the reason that you weren't able to work was an off짯the짯job illness or injury, the General Electric policy in Gilbert would have given you disability benefits. And what this Court said in the Gilbert case was that's not discrimination; it 짯짯 it simply doesn't include coverage for pregnancy, but pregnant women aren't fenced off. What this statutory text does is it says: No, employers have to treat pregnancy짯related conditions as favorably as they treat nonpregnancy짯related conditions. And that's in fact how this Court has read the statute since it's very first PDA case. In Newport News, what this Court said was that the Act makes clear that it's discriminatory to treat pregnancy짯related conditions less favorably than other medical conditions. And here UPS, with the three very large classes of employees that it provides accommodations to who are not pregnant, is treating pregnancy짯related conditions less favorably than other medical conditions.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: So you do assert it's a 짯짯 it's a most favored nation provision. You 짯짯 you have to give the benefits that you give to any other class of employees, right?

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: To any other class of employees, I think that's right.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: How long 짯짯 it doesn't matter whether that class is enormous or small, right?

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Well, I 짯짯 so I think this is 짯짯 this is text that obviously requires some degree of interpretation. I think Your Honor articulated one way of thinking about it, which is providing it 짯짯 providing this accommodation or benefit to employees generally. And certainly, when an employer provides accommodations or benefits to such large classes of employees who are not pregnant, who are similar in the ability to work 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Does 짯짯 does the record show what you have asserted here, that the classes that are given special treatment is almost everybody?

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Well, I think that we have 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Does the record show that?

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: I think 짯짯 I think the record is sufficient to show that. Remember, summary judgment was granted against us.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I understand.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Yes. So I think the record is sufficient to show that in the following sense: So, number one, obviously, there's all the on짯the짯job injuries, which 짯짯 which UPS acknowledges they provide accommodations for. Number two, DOT짯disqualifying conditions that UPS provides accommodated work for; we have presented examples in the record of individuals who have off짯the짯job injuries, who are similar in their ability to work as Peggy Young, who have been given accommodated work, and UPS hasn't pointed to in its briefing here any driver with a similar lifting restriction to Peggy Young who was not pregnant who didn't get the accommodation.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: You can win your case with that argument, perhaps.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: And I hope so, yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Yes, assume that. But that isn't going to help me, which I'm rather selfish about.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Because my 짯짯 my job here is to write what this statute means for a lot of cases. And writing the words, what it means is if you give a lot of benefits to a lot of employees, but not to the pregnant women and you don't give it to some employees and not to the pregnant women, and the employer says, look, pregnant women are like the few we don't give it to, not to the lot we do give it to, employer, you lose. But by the time I've written that into the U.S. Code, nobody knows what I'm talking about. Do you understand? I 짯짯 I need to know how to interpret the words such that they would do, in your view, what you want them to do, which is just what I said.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Yes. And so 짯짯 and I think the important point is if an employer provides accommodations as a matter of policy to a class of employees who are not pregnant, who are similar in their ability or inability to work to the pregnant plaintiff and does not provide the same accommodation or benefit to the pregnant plaintiff, it is violating the plain text of the statute, which says that women affected by pregnancy 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Most favored nation. So you're coming down to most favored nation. And that makes sense and that's easy for my colleague to describe. He can write that down in his opinion.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: But unfortunately, it takes out of what you just said the fact that you give them to a lot of employees. Because you could have a most favored nation that was two employees 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: 짯짯 including those who've only worked there for 4 years. A huge seniority. So 짯짯 so those are the words that I'm 짯짯

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: I understand. And 짯짯 and I understand. And that's why I think this may be an easier case than the one 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Basically what you're saying, if I understand it, is it's okay to differentiate on the basis of anything but source, which means whether it's work or non짯work짯related. That 짯짯 that's your 짯짯

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You're reading out of the legislative history the fact that Congress repeatedly said, we're not forcing employers to give benefits for non짯work짯related injuries, but we're going to write it so they have to anyway.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: So I 짯짯 so I don't think there's any statement in the legislative history that says we're not forcing employers to give benefits for non짯work짯related injuries. What 짯짯 there are three statements in the legislative history that 짯짯 that Respondent draws a negative inference from.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I'm so relieved.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: That Respondent draws a negative inference from to say, obviously, Congress didn't mean to do that. But to return to Justice Scalia's response there, I mean, the point is the text contains no such limitation. And on짯the짯job/off짯the짯job distinctions were certainly known to Congress at the time it adopted this 짯짯 it adopted this statute. In fact, General Electric v. Gilbert involved a policy that contained an on짯the짯job/off짯the짯job distinction, although the flip side of the one in this case. If Congress meant to say that employers have an exception from the general "shall be treated the same" requirement for an on짯the짯job/off짯the짯job distinction, it could have said so. If I might reserve the balance of my time.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: General Verrilli. SUPPORTING THE PETITIONER

turns.name: VERRILLI
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The point of the Pregnancy Discrimination Act is to reduce the number of women who are driven from the workforce or forced to go months without an income as a result of becoming pregnant. The second clause of the PDA advances that interest in a narrow but important way. I say the second clause is narrow because it is not a freestanding accommodation requirement like the religious provision of Title VII or like the ADA. And I say it is narrow because there's only one thing that an employer can't do when it affords benefits or accommodations. It can't draw distinctions that treat pregnancy짯related medical conditions worse than other conditions with comparable effects on ability to work.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: That wasn't the position that the government took in the U.S. Postal Service policy. We are told that the government defended a policy that is, for all intents and purposes, the same as United Parcel Service. And more than that, some briefs called Petitioner's position frivolous, contrived. That was the government's position. So will you explain how the government 짯짯 I suppose to this day, because the Postal Service still retains, as far as we know, the exclusion of pregnant women.

turns.name: VERRILLI
turns.text: Of course, Justice Ginsburg. It is correct that the Department of Justice defended the Postal Service practices against charges like those that Ms. Young makes in this case. That's correct. We acknowledge that in footnote 2 of our brief to this Court. Since then, however, the EEOC has issued guidance, and that's a very significant fact. Congress has charged the EEOC with authority to interpret this statute and with an authority to enforce it.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I thought we felt that we don't give deference to the EEOC.

turns.name: VERRILLI
turns.text: You don't give Chevron deference to the EEOC.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Oh.

turns.name: VERRILLI
turns.text: But the government has interests 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Oh, come one. So we give what 짯짯 what do you call the other kind of deference? I mean, gee, you give that to me even when 짯짯 even when I'm in dissent. I mean, that just means, you know, treat it for what it's worth.

turns.name: VERRILLI
turns.text: The EEOC sets the enforcement policy for the Federal sector with respect to this issue. That's a significant fact. We took it into consideration in deciding what the position of the United States should be.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Would your position here be the same if the 2014 guideline had not been adopted?

turns.name: VERRILLI
turns.text: We didn't take that position before the 2014 guideline had been adopted, Justice Kennedy. And I just don't know how to answer that question because we took the position in light of the guidance it adopted in 2014, which we do consider to be significant and we do have to weigh our interest as enforcer of the law as well as employer. We did so on a considered basis and we came to the judgment that we thought was the correct judgment about the meaning of the statute.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But we don't give you any more deference than we give the EEOC, though, right?

turns.name: VERRILLI
turns.text: Well, with respect to this, I do think that the Court's got to decide what the best reading of the statute is, that's right.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: What the best reading is, regardless of what you think.

turns.name: VERRILLI
turns.text: That's correct. And if I could turn to that, I think 짯짯 and I hopefully in doing so will answer your question, Justice Alito, and also yours, Justice Kagan. Here's why we think the statutory text 짯짯 and if 짯짯 maybe it would help if I restated what I think the rule is and then explain where the textual basis comes from. We think the one thing an employer can't do as a result of the second clause is draw distinctions that treat pregnancy짯related medical conditions worse than other conditions with comparable effects on ability to work. It's that single thing. And so seniority, full짯time work, different job classifications, all of those things would be permissible distinctions for an employer to make to differentiate among who gets benefits. Now, as for the textual basis, I'm looking at the 짯짯 at the statute here, which is 짯짯 we've got it at page 12 and 13 of our brief and it's also in the last page of the appendix to the petition. It says that 짯짯 what it says is that among the class of people who are comparable in their ability to work," in other words, similar in their ability or inability to work, as the statute says, women with a pregnancy짯related medical condition, in other words, women affected by pregnancy, childbirth, or related medical condition, as the statute says, can't be treated worse on the basis of their condition 짯짯 that's what we think "treated the same" means in the statute 짯짯 than other workers with non짯pregnancy짯related medical conditions that impose comparable limitations. Those are other persons not so affected.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Would you give me your interpolation again? You 짯짯 you altered the phrase and the words you added were?

turns.name: VERRILLI
turns.text: "On the basis of their condition." And the reason we think that that's the sensible and best reading of the statutory text is because this is focused on the condition and not the person.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, but you start 짯짯 you've got 짯짯 at the very beginning, you listed three things that you said were reasonable distinctions. Because the word I'd like you to focus on is "other workers" and the problem is which other workers? Because it is easy to construct hypothetical cases where the work 짯짯 the employer treats some other workers the same as the statute and doesn't others. And which distinctions are reasonable and which ones are not, and how do we tell?

turns.name: VERRILLI
turns.text: I'd like to make two points in response, Justice Breyer. The first about the nature of the Title VII claim and the second about the nature of this kind of an antidiscrimination provision. With respect to the first, I think it's helpful to differentiate between a direct claim of discrimination, disparate treatment, versus a claim proven through the McDonnell Douglas framework. Now, we think in order to prove a direct claim without going through the McDonnell Douglas burden짯shifting analysis, what you've got to show is that an employer offers an accommodation to a significant class of employees and that that accommodation fails the test I described earlier. It's got to be a significant class. Now, we think that's this case. We think that's going to be most cases. But in the kinds of examples that Your Honor identified, the one guy driving across the mountain, for example, I think you'd have two issues there: First, when it's one person, you're not going to be able to make a direct case. You go through McDonnell Douglas and the employer may well have an explanation for that accommodation that would take it outside the source of the disability limitation and mean there's no liability. And then with respect to that example there's a second point to be made, I think, which is that that person who has to drive the particularly dangerous route, for example, may just well be in a different job category and, therefore, not similar in ability or 짯짯

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: I would have thought it's those types of cases that present the starkest example of discrimination on the basis of pregnancy, the idiosyncratic one. Oh, well, he's doing this, yes, but he's doing that and then the pregnant woman comes in and says, ah, you know, that's not the same thing. I thought maybe it's the 짯짯 the sort of the isolated examples that would be particularly glaring in their discriminatory treatment.

turns.name: VERRILLI
turns.text: Well, Mr. Chief Justice, I guess what I would say about that is that you could certainly bring a McDonnell Douglas claim against an individual idiosyncratic difference, but then if the employer can show that the accommodation was granted to one not on the basis of a criterion that this sentence in the PDA would forbid, then the employer's 짯짯 then the 짯짯 then the accommodation is fine, there's no violation. And to get back to the point 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That's the 짯짯 that's 짯짯 I used the idiosyncratic example, not because I'm interested in it, because I think it illustrates something that isn't idiosyncratic. And what I use it to illustrate is the fact that, as here, employers will have classes of people and the classes may be based on all kinds of different things. But this is a case where there are classes and some get the benefits equivalent to the pregnancy and some don't. And how are we supposed to tell which are the criteria that are consistent with the statute and which are not? That's what I found as the difficult question in the case. And that's why I ask it using the idiosyncratic, simply to illustrate what I think is the problem.

turns.name: VERRILLI
turns.text: Yes, of course. Let me get to the second point I wanted to make in response to your question, and then I'll try, after I do that, to give you a very specific response to what you just asked me. The second point is, you know, it is true that some classes are going to be in and some classes are going to be out, but that's how discrimination law operates. If an employer is discriminating against women in promotions, the fact that an employer is also discriminating against overweight men in promotions doesn't make the discrimination against women any less actionable because it just reflects the choice Congress made about whom to protect and whom not to protect. And here, the choice Congress made on whom to protect and whom not to protect is the choice to protect women who have conditions 짯짯 pregnancy짯related medical conditions. That's the congressional judgment here. They didn't choose to protect everybody who gets injured off the job. They chose to protect those with pregnancy짯related medical conditions.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Suppose the employer has the rule, we will, if you have a disability acquired outside of employment, give you benefits for one month, and it applies that same policy to the 짯짯 to the pregnant woman. Is that a violation of the statute?

turns.name: VERRILLI

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: But nothing less.

turns.name: VERRILLI
turns.text: Correct. That's correct.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Why isn't that discrimination on the basis of 짯짯

turns.name: VERRILLI
turns.text: Well, because the statute requires that people be treated the same, and so she would be. The pregnant employee would be treated the same under those circumstances. It doesn't require any 짯짯

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: But not if there is a separate category of people who are entitled to benefits for more than one month.

turns.name: VERRILLI
turns.text: Well, the question would be whether those benefits 짯짯 whether the distinction 짯짯 whether the disentitlement of the pregnant employee was based on the source of her condition, namely, pregnancy. If it's based on something else like seniority or full짯time status, then, of course 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Would you please answer my question, which was: Do you mean "source" means on the job and off the job? Is that what this case revolves around? Because I don't know what "source" means otherwise.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Briefly.

turns.name: VERRILLI
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Chief Justice, briefly. So I think that on the job versus off the job, that distinction goes to 짯짯 inevitably goes to the source of the impairment. And, of course, pregnancy will never qualify under that standard. But this case is not just about on the job versus off the job. It's about on the job versus off the job plus the DOT certification category, which can include people who lose their DOT certification and can't drive as a result of physical conditions other than pregnancy that prevent them from doing the job they have to do, which could include lifting. And the DOT manual, which the Petitioner cites at pages 6 and 7, says exactly that. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, General. Ms. Halligan.

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Justice Breyer, you are exactly correct. Had Petitioner believed that the policy that UPS applied, which was to provide accommodations to employees who are injured on the job but not to provide accommodations to any employees who sustained a condition incurred off the job, she could have brought a disparate impact claim. We believe she would not have succeeded, but she could have and she did not. She attempted to bring one late in the day. It was dismissed by the district court because it had not been exhausted.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, Ms. Halligan, could we talk about the claim that she did bring?

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: So your reading of the statute basically makes everything after the semicolon completely superfluous. And I think you would agree with that, wouldn't you?

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: Absolutely not, Your Honor. The reading that we propose is very straightforward. What Congress said in the second clause, the key words are "the same as other persons." What "other" means is simply distinct from whatever is mentioned first. So employers have to treat pregnant employees the same as some distinct group of nonpregnant employees that are similar in their ability or inability to work and that's exactly what UPS's policy is.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But that is what the first provision does. When it says pregnancy is the same as sex, when we say because of sex, we also say because of pregnancy, all of that would be taken care of by that clause.

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: This Court explained in Newport News, as well as in CalFed, that the function of the second clause is to explain how Title VII principles apply to pregnancy. And the reason that they had to do that was in order to repudiate the logic 짯짯

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: So 짯짯 so you are saying it's not doing anything new; it's only explaining the old stuff. And okay. Tell me why that's necessary?

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: I'm not 짯짯 I'm not saying that, Your Honor. What I'm saying is that in a pregnancy discrimination case, instead of comparing women with men as you would in a typical sex discrimination case, because what the first clause does is bolt pregnancy on to sex discrimination. And so if you compare women and men in a pregnancy discrimination case where you have a policy that facially discriminates against pregnancy, you will nonetheless conclude that there's not sex discrimination because there will be women who are pregnant in the disfavored group, but there will also be women who are not pregnant in the favored group, along with men

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I think, again, that that is not necessary, because all that the inquiry would be is were you discriminated against because you were pregnant? Yes, I was. No, I wasn't. You don't need any of this other stuff about what the comparator class is. And, in fact, you are creating a kind of double redundancy. It's everything past the semicolon is redundant, but then, moreover, the key words here, which is "other persons not so affected but similar in their ability or inability to work," that becomes redundant even within the redundancy.

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: I think, to respond to the last point first and then to the first. What Petitioner's interpretation and the government's interpretation would do would actually be to rewrite those words in one of two ways. Initially, Petitioner seemed to be suggesting that if a plaintiff could identify any other single employee who was accommodated that the pregnant employee would be entitled to the same accommodation. What that would mean is the statute would have to read "the same as any other person." It does not. Now Petitioner and the government are both suggesting that the only restriction that this bars is a restriction based on source. Any other restriction, rank, seniority status, outside legal obligations, are acceptable. But it doesn't contain any of those words either, not "source," not 짯짯

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: That seems the 짯짯 that is the question that this language raises, right? Which is why source? But why not a seniority limitation or something like that? Could I give you an alternative way to understand what the statute is doing? Which is that 짯짯 what we ought to be thinking about is McDonnell Douglas. In other words, this 짯짯 this provides the comparator. It says an employee can find a class of people who are being given an accommodation notwithstanding that those people are similarly situated with respect to work. An employee points to that class. And then in a typical McDonnell Douglas fashion, the employer comes back and says: No, there is a good reason why I'm treating that class differently that has nothing to do with pregnancy. It has something to do with I always treat more senior employees differently or something like that. And at that point, if the 짯짯 if the employer makes his case, the employee gets to come back and say, no, that is a pretext, in just the way we do with every other discrimination case. And that's what this is all about. It's identifying the comparator that the employee has to identify in the first instance in order to shift the burden to the government to come back with a reason.

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: I think the second clause is highly relevant to the question of comparators, but not in the way that you are suggesting. What the second clause does, as this Court has laid out in Newport News and in CalFed, is to explain when you are making those comparisons that you don't look at women and men, which is what you might do, as this Court did in Gilbert, because it's sex discrimination that you are actually classifying 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: That's the first clause. But, I mean, instead of talking in the abstract, can you give me any example of a case that a plaintiff would lose under the first clause that puts pregnancy together with sex.

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: I'm not sure that you could, but that wasn't the function of the second clause. And Petitioner 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Then you are saying second clause adds nothing even though Congress said "and." There is one clause because of sex, includes pregnancy, "and" something in addition. But you are saying it's not really in addition.

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: I think that grammatical connector is very important in understanding how the two clauses relate, for the following reason: Petitioner's construction would read the first clause out of the statute entirely. The words in the first clause are "because of." And this Court has consistently understood those words across protected traits to require that discrimination 짯짯 in an intentional discrimination case, that you have discrimination that is actually motivated by the protected trait. If the second clause does the work Petitioner suggests, even if you could find the word "source" in that where it's not in the text, it would mean that you don't need to show that the protected trait, pregnancy, actually motivated the adverse treatment. So his construction would read that out of the statute entirely.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Ms. Halligan, what is wrong with my middle ground? It's not that Mr. Bagenstos and the General's ground, because it allows the employer to come back and say: I have a legitimate policy based on seniority, or even I have a legitimate policy based on the source of the injury. But it does put that as a question whenever an employee is able to point to a similar 짯짯 to a class of people who are granted the disability accommodation who aren't pregnant.

turns.name: HALLIGAN

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN

What is that doing? What it does is it points to the comparator that sets off the McDonnell Douglas test, that forces the employer to come back and give a reason for why it is that this ought not to be taken as discrimination against pregnancy.

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: I think that this Court's been clear that the function of the second clause is to repudiate that logic which equates 짯짯 when you look at women and men and you have a pregnancy 짯짯 a cause 짯짯 a policy that discriminates on the basis of pregnancy, you say that is not sex discrimination. What that would also do is to collapse the distinction between disparate treatment and disparate intent. This Court has been clear that that is an absolute line. It said so in Raytheon. Congress tracked that distinction in the 1991 Civil Rights Act, and Justice Stevens in his dissent in Gilbert itself which this Court said it was codifying when it enacted the pregnancy discrimination 짯짯

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: I think the language after the semicolon instructs that when you look at a policy that facially discriminates on the basis of pregnancy, what you would typically do in a sex discrimination case is to look at how women and men are treated. And if they are treated differently, you would conclude that there is sex discrimination. What this clause instructs is that when you look at a policy that discriminates on the basis of pregnancy, rather than looking at women and men which would lead you to the conclusion that there is no sex discrimination 짯짯 because all the non짯pregnant women 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: That's what the first clause does. It says pregnancy and sex, period. You have already said that you don't think that the second clause does any practical work. That is, you can't conceive of a case where a plaintiff would loose under clause one and win under clause two.

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: To be clear, Your Honor, the reason the second clause is there is to avoid a case in which a court uses the same reasoning and reaches a different result. This Court also attached special significance to the second clause in Johnson Controls. It said that the second clause provides a BFOQ for pregnancy specifically, and so it does that work as well. What Petitioner suggests is that the second clause somehow permits any distinction except on짯the짯job versus off짯the짯job. That's a distinction that is longstanding and hasn't 짯짯

And now a distinction is being made. The employer says, no, you are not because you didn't drive over the mountain pass. Or, no, you are not because you got it off the job. And then we have to decide is that a pretext? Is it legitimate? And where they are giving it to everybody else and there are very few, it doesn't sound too legitimate. But that test must come in.

turns.name: HALLIGAN

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: And so 짯짯 and so how does it and does it matter if we put it under the first so짯called whatever, you know, intentional as opposed to disparate impact? Will we muck up the law were we to say it goes in that part rather than the other part or both parts?

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: Well, I 짯짯 I think if I can, this Court has been clear that McDonnell Douglas provides a mechanism for providing indirect evidence of disparate treatment of intentional discrimination. So it's distinct, I think, from a disparate impact case where, as here, you have a facially neutral policy, a policy that says on짯the짯job gets accommodation when they can't perform the essential functions of their job, anyone with an injury or condition that's sustained off the job doesn't. And what a 짯짯 what a plaintiff who believed that nonetheless there was intentional discrimination afoot, what they would do is they would, under McDonnell Douglas, they would, first of all, attempt to make a prime facia case by showing that other employees who were similarly situated were being treated differently. The comparators that the Petitioner points to here are not valid because they're not similarly situated. The bottom line 짯짯

But the Fourth Circuit said, yes, that's what it says just standing alone, but because it would lead to untoward results, preferential treatment, we're not going to give it 짯짯

turns.name: HALLIGAN

Justice Kagan, to go back to your question, what an employee could do in that circumstance is to say, the policy doesn't treat similarly situated employees the same as me. It treats me worse. The comparators here were not at all congruous. The first set of comparators were individuals who were accommodated under the ADA. The government realizes that they're not similar 짯짯

turns.text: But that's where we disagree because 짯짯 because what this tells you is it tells you what the comparators are. The comparators are any class you can come up with who is 짯짯 is 짯짯 has the same disability and isn't pregnant, and then the employer can come back and say, no, we had a good reason to 짯짯 to treat that class of employees differently. And if you 짯짯 if you buy that with respect to the Gilbert distinction, I don't understand why you wouldn't buy it with respect to any other classification.

turns.name: HALLIGAN

It was simply to correct the fact that pregnancy could be sex discrimination. So all 짯짯

It said it's illegitimate in the first clause and it said you cannot, when you are trying to ascertain if there's sex discrimination, with a pregnancy policy, break it down into women and men because you won't get the result Congress wants. Congress says when it's facially discriminatory on the basis of pregnancy, that's sex discrimination. So the comparators do have to be different; you are correct. It's pregnant employees and nonpregnant employees. And 짯짯 and 짯짯

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Is there really a dispute about this? Maybe Petitioner's counsel could address it in rebuttal, but is there really a dispute that if a UPS driver fell off his all짯terrain vehicle during 짯짯 on the weekend and was unable to lift that that person would not be given light duty? Is there really a 짯짯

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: There's no dispute at all and the District Court made a square finding exactly to that effect at page 56A and page 35A. I would also direct you in our red brief to page 5 where we set forth Ms. Martin's testimony that she never authorized an accommodation for anyone who was injured off the job, so that's there as well. I'd like to turn briefly, if I can, to the question of the EEOC guidance that the solicitor general 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I 짯짯 I 짯짯 but there are individuals who are injured off the job who lose their DOT licenses?

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: There are individuals who lose their DOT certification and pursuant to the collective bargaining agreement, they are accommodated for some period of time. But those jobs, the individuals who lose their DOT certification, are not light짯duty jobs. Those are heavy짯lifting jobs, as the District Court squarely held. The District Court at page 36A and 59A said, "Inside jobs are not light짯duty jobs and the individuals who lose their license can perform any number of demanding physical tasks," which Ms. Young could not perform. So they're not comparable in that regard either. With respect to the EEOC guidance, the guidance which was issued two weeks after this Court granted certiorari is 180짯degree change from the position that the government has consistently taken and that the postal service, which UPS fairly looked to in trying to ascertain what appropriate conduct was under federal antidiscrimination laws, the policy that it still has in place today. In addition, the process in issuing that guidance was incredibly rushed. It was not until 2012, as one of the amicus briefs point out, that the EEOC even identified the question of pregnancy accommodations as an emerging or developing issue. There was no notice and comment. The three 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: The original 짯짯 the original guideline, as I understand EEOC, what they did in 2014, they said, we were terse the first time around. All we're doing in 2014 is explaining that what the original 짯짯 what was 짯짯 it was '79, the original 짯짯

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: '79 guidelines, the '79 guidelines simply mimic the language of the statute. In 2012 the EEOC, in its strategic plan, said that it was looking at addressing the very issue that it opined on in the 2014 guidance as emerging. If the 1979 guidelines stood for what Petitioner suggests, there would have been no need to treat it as emerging. It would have been settled 30 years ago. Finally, I want to point out that this is an area where the democratic process is working as it should and as this Court instructed it should in Cal Fed. In Cal Fed, this Court looked at the question of whether or not state statutes which provided preferential treatment to pregnant employees, the statute there provided extra leave and reinstatement rights to pregnant employees, was preempted by the PDA. The Court said the PDA sets a floor. That floor is that you can't single out pregnancy for adverse treatment. States can go beyond that as additional and new challenges are identified.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, Ms. Halligan, for the democratic process to work as it should, the PDA has to be given a fair reading. And what we know about the PDA is that it was supposed to be about removing stereotypes of pregnant women as marginal workers. It was supposed to be about ensuring that they wouldn't be unfairly excluded from the workplace. And what you are saying is that there's a policy that accommodates some workers, but puts all pregnant women on one side of the line. And what you are further saying is that the employer doesn't even have to justify that policy ala McDonnell Douglas. That seems to me a reading of the statute, the PDA, that ignores two짯thirds of the text.

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: I'm not saying that the employer isn't subject to a suit under McDonnell Douglas. I'm saying that there are no valid comparators here. That's 짯짯 that's all 짯짯 all that we're saying in that regard. The states that 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: So essentially it says any group that doesn't get the benefit, a group that is non짯pregnant, then pregnant people are 짯짯 any group at all?

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: If you had a policy, I'm not sure what one would look like, that singled out pregnant employees plus one other employee, my guess is that you'd find 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: What category of employees?

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: The policy that's at issue here, Justice Ginsburg, distinguishes on짯the짯job versus off짯the짯job injuries. That's a distinction that's echoed in state and in federal law. That's a far cry from a policy that singles out pregnant women. There are nine states that 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Singling out is in the first 짯짯 is what the first 짯짯

turns.name: HALLIGAN
turns.text: Or targeting or otherwise primarily disadvantaging. That distinction tracks what workers' comp requires, which is payment for employees who are injured on the job, and many employers, including the U.S. Postal Service, have found it advantageous to provide light짯duty accommodations so their employees can be at work while they are rehabilitating and provide some productive work for the company. That distinction is as legitimate as you could get. I see my time is up, Your Honor.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Bagenstos, you have four minutes remaining.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice. So I'd like to begin, if I could, with the facts, because Justice Alito did ask, and, yes, we certainly do disagree with UPS's 짯짯 UPS's assertion here. This case was on summary judgment and UPS does point correctly to some very general statements in the record by UPS managers that they never authorized these accommodations. However, we point to specific examples in the record of people with off짯the짯job injuries or illnesses who were DOT decertified who were given accommodations, and not just accommodations that remove them from driving but also remove them from heavy lifting. That's a factual dispute that has to go to trial.

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: You really think that you could prove at trial that if somebody is injured in a recreational activity over the weekend that they get light duty but a pregnant women does not maybe?

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: So if someone is injured over the weekend in a way that leads them to be DOT decertified, yes, and in fact, the UPS manager so testified about his sports injury. We cite that in our opening brief. So yes, we think so. The second point I'd like to make is about what the two clauses do, and I think this is very important. So the first clause of the PDA, as this Court has said in Newport News and Cal Fed, overturns the reasoning in General Electric v. Gilbert. So what the first clause says is where Gilbert said, look, discrimination based on pregnancy isn't sex discrimination because there are pregnant women and non짯pregnant persons, that's wrong instead because a pregnancy is because of sex definitionally. That's not what the second clause does, that's what the first clause does. The second clause, as this Court said again in Newport News and Cal Fed, goes further and overrules the holding. And I think Justice Kagan was exactly correct in describing the facts of Gilbert, that the Gilbert holding would not be overturned by 짯짯 under UPS's reading here because the Gilbert policy, the one thing we know that Congress meant to say was illegal, the Gilbert policy itself acted, drew lines in pregnancy짯neutral ways. It said if you have an off짯the짯job injury or accident, defined as an off짯the짯job illness or accident defined as an accidental injury, then you get disability benefits. It just so happens pregnancy isn't an illness and pregnancy isn't an accident in the sense of an accidental injury. And what Congress 짯짯 we know Congress was trying to do, because Congress said it and this Court has said it, is to overturn the holding there. But UPS's rule simply reprices the rule at issue in Gilbert. If I might return to the point Justice Breyer's made a couple of times at various points in the argument짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Actually I think the reverse. The second sentence is what does that. The second sentence says you don't worry about whether it's between sexes. You worry about whether the same class of people, people who are injured off짯duty, are being treated differently when they have the same ability to work.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Well, I think, Justice Sotomayor, the first clause says you don't worry about whether they're the same sex or not. You don't look at 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, you do have to worry about it because it still has to be sex discrimination.

turns.name: BAGENSTOS
turns.text: Well, no. But the first clause definitionally defines pregnancy discrimination as sex discrimination. It says if you're discriminating because of pregnancy, that is because of sex. And that's the 짯짯 that's overturning the Gilbert reasoning coming from Geduldig that pregnancy discrimination isn't sex discrimination. The second clause goes further, as this Court's explained, and overturns the holding, overturns the holding upholding the General Electric policy. And so 짯짯 and I think under 짯짯 under UPS's rule it wouldn't do that. On Justice Breyer's question, basically how do we deal with a world where there's an employer that treats two different groups of people who are non짯pregnant differently? Does "shall be treated the same" mean shall be treated the same as those who get the better deal or those who get the worst deal; right? And I think Justice Ginsburg and Justice Kagan I think articulated this well, that their position really would give least favored nation status to pregnant workers and we know that that can't be something that Congress intended. We know that in part because of what General Verrilli said, that that's not how anti짯discrimination law works, the fact that someone else was discriminated against doesn't mean I lose. Justice Alito's opinion for the Third Circuit in the Fraternal Order of Police of Newark case articulates the same rule. We know that as well because the purpose of this statute is to say to employers, as Justice Kagan said, you have to treat pregnant workers as just as valued employees as anybody else, and if you think it's valuable to keep these employees on the job who are injured on the job because they keep valuable work 짯짯 valuable knowledge within the company, do that for pregnant women. Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
